StartupMessage: First message on the wire. Tells the server which database, user, and options the client wants — and implicitly, which protocol version.
AuthenticationRequest: Server's challenge before any data flows. Tells the client whether (and how) to prove identity.
AuthenticationOK: Server has accepted the credentials. ParameterStatus and BackendKeyData follow, then ReadyForQuery.
ParameterStatus: Server proactively shares a setting the client may care about (server_version, application_name, client_encoding, ...). Sent unsolicited at startup, and again whenever any of these change.
BackendKeyData: Server identifies its backend process and shares the secret needed to cancel a long-running query later. Sent once, at startup.
ReadyForQuery: Server is idle and ready for the next command. The status code (I/T/E) tells the client whether a transaction is open.
Query: Simple-query protocol: one SQL string, one result stream. No parameters, no preparation, no cursor.
Parse: Step 1 of the extended-query flow. Asks the server to validate and prepare a SQL statement, optionally naming it for reuse.
Bind: Step 2 of the extended-query flow. Pairs a prepared statement with concrete parameter values and result format codes, creating a portal.
Describe: Step 3 of the extended-query flow (optional). Asks the server to describe a prepared statement's parameter types, or a portal's result columns.
Execute: Step 4 of the extended-query flow. Runs the portal and streams back rows.
Sync: Step 5 of the extended-query flow. Tells the server 'I'm done with this batch' — server responds with ReadyForQuery and resyncs error state.
RowDescription: Server announces the schema of the result rows that are about to follow. Sent before the first DataRow of a result set.
DataRow: One row of result values. Comes after RowDescription, before CommandComplete.
CommandComplete: Marks the end of a successful statement. The tag tells the client what kind of operation finished (SELECT N, INSERT 0 N, UPDATE N, ...).
ErrorResponse: Server reports a failure. After ErrorResponse, the server will send ReadyForQuery and discard the rest of any extended-query batch.
Terminate: Client gracefully closes the connection. No reply expected.
ParseComplete: Backend confirmation that the prior Parse succeeded. The named prepared statement (or the unnamed one) is now known to the server.
BindComplete: Backend confirmation that the prior Bind succeeded. The portal now exists with concrete parameter values and is ready for Execute.
CloseComplete: Backend confirmation that a Close request (against a portal or prepared statement) succeeded. The server has dropped the named object.
Close: Frontend request to drop a portal or a prepared statement by name. Frees server-side resources without closing the connection.
Flush: Frontend hint: 'flush any pending output now.' Lets the client read partial results during a long pipelined batch, without ending the batch the way Sync would.
NoData: Backend reply to Describe when the target will produce no result rows (DML without RETURNING, DDL, ...). Replaces RowDescription in that case.
EmptyQueryResponse: Backend response to a Query whose SQL string was empty (or whitespace/comments only). Substitutes for RowDescription/CommandComplete.
ParameterDescription: Backend response to a Describe-statement request, listing the OIDs of the parameters the prepared statement expects. Sent before any RowDescription/NoData.
PortalSuspended: Backend indication that an Execute hit its row limit before the result set was exhausted. The portal is still open; the client can Execute again to keep reading.
NoticeResponse: Server reports a non-fatal notice (warning, info, debug). Can arrive unsolicited at almost any time — the client must be ready to receive it between any two normal messages.
NotificationResponse: Server delivers a LISTEN/NOTIFY event from another backend. Arrives asynchronously; only emitted after the client has issued LISTEN for the channel.
NegotiateProtocolVersion: Server tells the client it speaks an older protocol minor version (or doesn't recognize some startup options). Sent in response to StartupMessage when not all options were honoured.
CopyInResponse: Backend signals 'I'm ready to receive bulk-load data.' Sent in response to COPY ... FROM STDIN. Triggers the CopyData/CopyDone exchange in the frontend direction.
CopyOutResponse: Backend signals 'I'm about to stream bulk-export data.' Sent in response to COPY ... TO STDOUT. CopyData rows follow until CopyDone.
CopyBothResponse: Backend signals a bidirectional COPY (replication only). Both sides may send CopyData until either ends with CopyDone.
CopyData: One chunk of COPY payload (load or export). Frame size is up to the sender; receivers must concatenate to reconstruct the data stream.
CopyDataBinaryHeader: First chunk of a binary-format COPY stream. Carries the PostgreSQL binary-file header: an 11-byte magic signature (PGCOPY\n\377\r\n\0), a 32-bit critical-flags field (reserved, must be 0 in PG 12+), a 32-bit header-extension length, and any extension bytes. See https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-copy.html.
CopyDataBinaryTrailer: Two-byte end-of-stream marker (FF FF) that closes a binary-format COPY stream. The next message on the wire is the matching CopyDone from the sender.
CopyDataBinary: One chunk of binary-format COPY payload. Inside the stream, the wire layout is per-tuple: int16 field count, then per-field int32 length (-1 = NULL) + raw bytes. Tuples may span chunks.
CopyDone: Sender signals end-of-stream for a COPY exchange. Server then sends CommandComplete and ReadyForQuery.
CopyFail: Frontend aborts an in-progress COPY ... FROM with an error message. Server sends ErrorResponse and ReadyForQuery in reply.
FunctionCall: Frontend fast-path call to a built-in function by OID. Legacy mechanism, deprecated; modern code uses SELECT or extended-query instead.
FunctionCallResponse: Backend reply to a FunctionCall, carrying the single return value.
Password: Frontend reply carrying credentials in response to AuthenticationMD5Password or AuthenticationCleartextPassword. (For SCRAM, SASLInitialResponse/SASLResponse are used instead.)
SSLRequest: Frontend pseudo-message asking 'does this server accept TLS?' before sending StartupMessage. The server replies with a single byte: 'S' (yes) or 'N' (no).
SSLResponse: Server's single-byte verdict on the prior SSLRequest. 'S' = accept (client should immediately begin TLS handshake), 'N' = reject (client decides whether to fall back to cleartext or give up).
GSSENCRequest: Frontend pseudo-message asking 'does this server accept GSSAPI encryption?'. Same 8-byte shape as SSLRequest but with request code 80877104. Npgsql 10 sends this even when SslMode=Disable.
GSSENCResponse: Server's single-byte verdict on the prior GSSENCRequest. 'G' = accept, 'N' = reject. After a reject, the client typically falls back to a normal StartupMessage on the same connection.
CancelRequest: Frontend message sent on a fresh TCP connection (not the in-flight one) to ask the server to cancel a running query. Carries the (pid, secret) pair the server published earlier in BackendKeyData. The server has no obligation to acknowledge; the cancel is best-effort.
